subject: Psychology
topic: Cycle A Foundations assessment and marking guide

instructional_contract.learning_target: Students demonstrate foundational psychology reasoning by defining concepts, using evidence, and avoiding overclaims.
do: name evidence needed for a claim
explain why correlation does not prove causation
identify an ethical issue in a simple research design
instructional_contract.understand: Psychological claims require careful evidence, cautious interpretation, and ethical research practices.

assessment.title: Cycle A Foundations Assessment
instructions: Use course vocabulary where it helps.
Do not diagnose anyone or make claims that the evidence does not support.

sections.label: Part A — Key Vocabulary
prompts: Explain why evidence matters when psychologists study behaviour or mental processes.
Write one observation and one possible inference from a neutral classroom statement.

sections.label: Part B — Claim and Evidence
prompts: Explain what a claim about studying with music is saying.
Name evidence needed before accepting the claim.
Explain one reason the claim might be too simple.

sections.label: Part C — Correlation and Causation
prompts: Identify two related variables in a sleep-and-focus survey.
Explain why a survey relationship does not prove a cause.

sections.label: Part D — Research Ethics
prompts: Name one ethical or fairness concern in a simple classroom research design.
Suggest one way to make the design more fair or responsible.

sections.label: Part E — Scenario Application
prompts: Write a careful explanation using evidence, correlation, and possible cause.
Write one limit statement naming what cannot be known from the scenario alone.

student_checklist: I explained correlation and causation carefully.

marking_guide.title: Cycle A Foundations Marking Guide

entries.look_for: Psychology as the scientific study of behaviour and mental processes.
Evidence supports claims, tests ideas, and helps avoid guessing.
Observation is directly visible; inference is a possible explanation that is not proven.
A survey relationship does not prove causation because other variables could be involved.
Ethical concerns may include fairness, consent, privacy, or avoiding disadvantage.

scoring_guidance: Use the rubric to reward cautious reasoning and evidence language.
Do not require terminology beyond the Cycle A target.
Credit accurate limit statements even when phrasing is simple.
